coverage_criteria: Provides their current role or title, such as job title, academic position, or equivalent.

subtopics.description: Week Recall: the participant's initial description of what their typical week looks like, establishing the base task list.
coverage_criteria: STEP 1 (week recall) — REQUIRED: The participant has described what their typical week of work looks like, including the kinds of tasks they actually do. This is satisfied if they described their day-to-day or weekly work pattern in ANY prior response — do not re-ask if it was already answered during role/context. Ask a week-reconstruction question (e.g., 'Can you walk me through what a typical week looks like?', 'What did you work on yesterday?') only if no such description exists yet. The question MUST be anchored in time and actions — do NOT ask about responsibilities, ownership, or role identity (e.g., do NOT ask 'what are your core responsibilities', 'what's really yours vs the lab's', 'how do you see your role'). Those belong to Role and Context, not Task Inventory.

subtopics.description: Task List Completion: sweeping for lower-cadence tasks, confirming the list is exhaustive, and ensuring every task has a stated objective.
coverage_criteria: STEP 2 (frequency sweep) — REQUIRED: The Interviewer has explicitly asked about tasks that occur on a LOWER cadence — monthly, quarterly, or a few times a year. The question MUST reference cadence/frequency (e.g., 'Is there anything you do monthly or every few months that wouldn't show up in a typical week?'). A generic 'anything else?' does NOT satisfy this step. Ask STEP 2 exactly once — after it is asked, never re-ask it or a variant of it.

STEP 3 (targeted catch-all + completeness confirmation) — CONDITIONAL: After STEP 2, the Interviewer has asked a completeness confirmation. If there are obvious role-typical tasks missing from the inventory, this question must name those specific expected tasks AND end with a completeness confirmation (e.g., 'I haven't heard you mention advising students or grant writing — are any of those part of your work, or does that basically cover everything?'). If nothing obvious is missing, ask ONLY the completeness confirmation (e.g., 'Does that basically cover everything you spend your time on?'). STEP 3 cannot be combined with STEP 2. Ask it exactly once; a 'no' or 'yes' answer is final.

COMPLETION: Once STEP 1 and STEP 2 have been asked, and STEP 3 has been asked once or deliberately skipped, this subtopic is FULLY COVERED. Do NOT re-probe, do NOT ask another version of STEP 2 or STEP 3, and do NOT reopen the task list for any reason. If the user gives two consecutive sub-5-word replies with no new task information, stop probing and treat this as done.

Every task in the inventory has a stated objective — the participant has described WHY they do it (the goal or outcome, not the procedural steps). If a task was mentioned without a purpose, the interviewer has asked for it before moving on. Tasks with only an action+object and no stated purpose are NOT covered.

subtopics.description: Time allocation: how much time they spend on each task, as a percentage of their total work week. A time-split widget is shown to the participant to collect the actual percentages. IMPORTANT: The interviewer MUST send a message targeting this subtopic — for example, 'Now let's see how your time breaks down across these tasks — here's a quick widget for you to fill in.' This message triggers the widget. Do NOT ask for per-task percentages one by one in conversation. Do NOT skip this subtopic even if task-list completeness was already confirmed in the Breadth subtopic.
coverage_criteria: PREREQUISITE: The task list has been confirmed complete. This is satisfied by EITHER: (a) STEP 3 of the Breadth subtopic, which always ends with a completeness confirmation (e.g., 'Does that basically cover everything you spend your time on?') and the participant did not introduce a large new unaccounted block of time in reply, OR (b) a standalone completeness confirmation asked by the Interviewer that received a similar response. Do NOT ask another completeness question if STEP 3 already covered it. If the task list is not yet confirmed complete, ask the completeness question first.

The participant has provided explicit percentage or fraction breakdowns showing how their work time is divided across tasks — for example, 'I spend about 50% on X and 30% on Y', or a submitted widget with per-task percentages. Evaluate this criterion by scanning the conversation for explicit numeric time splits: if NO percentages or fractions appear in the participant's messages, this criterion is FALSE regardless of any other confirmations. A 'yes' or 'no that's it' reply to a completeness question does NOT satisfy this criterion.
Every task in the Task Inventory has a corresponding time allocation entry. This is FALSE if the participant has not quantified time for each task.
